coredata.dc:title: Discrimination and detection of benzaldehyde derivatives using sensor array based on fluorescent carbon nanodots
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Yingying
Liu, Xue
Wu, Qiuhua
Yi, Jie
Zhang, Guolin
coredata.dc:description: Excessive residual benzaldehyde derivatives in environment can cause severe ecological problems. There are currently less simple, rapid, sensitive and low-cost methods available to detect benzaldehyde derivatives. In this study, fluorescent nano-probes based on carbon nanodots (CDs) were developed for the determination of benzaldehyde derivatives in aqueous solution. Three kinds of classical CDs with different structure and photoluminescence characteristic were randomly chosen and prepared by using different carbon source and synthetic methods Static fluorescent quenching occurred to CDs in the presence of benzaldehyde derivatives. The concentration detection range covered from μM level to mM level, and the minimum detection limit reached 0.3 μM. Benzaldehyde derivatives with various structures and functional groups contributed differential fluorescent quenching degrees to CDs through the donor-acceptor electronic effect. On this basis, sensor array based on CDs was constructed for the discrimination and determination of benzaldehyde derivatives. Two principal components contributing 99.22% of the total variability were extracted by using principal component analysis method (PCA). PCA scattergraph was plotted and applied to descriminate five benzaldehyde derivatives intuitively, including 2,4-dihydroxybenzaldehyde, salicylaldehyde, m-hydroxybenzaldehyde, benzaldehyde and m-methylbenzaldehyde. Experimental results confirmed the practical utility and stability of the CD array for the assay of benzaldehyde derivatives.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sensor array
Fluorescence
Carbon nanodots
Principal component analysis

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref vitae

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: PL response of CDs to benzaldehyde derivatives

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the sensing mechanism of carbon nanodots (CDs) to benzaldehyde derivatives.
The XPS spectra and high-resolution XPS C 1s and N 1s spectra of CDs.
The PL spectra of CDs under different excitation wavelengths.
The PL intensity of CDs in the presence of various benzaldehyde derivatives (10 mM).

Left column: the fluorescence response of CDs to various benzaldehyde derivatives with the concentration of 0–50 mM. Right column: the linear fitting plots of the normalized fluorescence intensity of CDs to the concentration of benzaldehyde derivatives.
(A) Fingerprints of benzaldehyde derivatives generated by the CD sensor array. (B) Scattergraph of benzaldehyde derivatives generated by PCA.
(A) Double blind experiments to identify the unknown benzaldehyde derivatives (BD-1 and BD-2). (B) Background interference experiments in local running water.
The elemental content of the carbon source and their resulted CDs.

head.ce:title: Discrimination and detection of benzaldehyde derivatives using sensor array based on fluorescent carbon nanodots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Liaoning Province Key Laboratory for Green Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry of Advanced Materials, College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, Shenyang, 110036, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Liaoning Province Key Laboratory for Green Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry of Advanced Materials

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Excessive residual benzaldehyde derivatives in environment can cause severe ecological problems. There are currently less simple, rapid, sensitive and low-cost methods available to detect benzaldehyde derivatives. In this study, fluorescent nano-probes based on carbon nanodots (CDs) were developed for the determination of benzaldehyde derivatives in aqueous solution. Three kinds of classical CDs with different structure and photoluminescence characteristic were randomly chosen and prepared by using different carbon source and synthetic methods Static fluorescent quenching occurred to CDs in the presence of benzaldehyde derivatives. The concentration detection range covered from μM level to mM level, and the minimum detection limit reached 0.3 μM. Benzaldehyde derivatives with various structures and functional groups contributed differential fluorescent quenching degrees to CDs through the donor-acceptor electronic effect. On this basis, sensor array based on CDs was constructed for the discrimination and determination of benzaldehyde derivatives. Two principal components contributing 99.22% of the total variability were extracted by using principal component analysis method (PCA). PCA scattergraph was plotted and applied to descriminate five benzaldehyde derivatives intuitively, including 2,4-dihydroxybenzaldehyde, salicylaldehyde, m-hydroxybenzaldehyde, benzaldehyde and m-methylbenzaldehyde. Experimental results confirmed the practical utility and stability of the CD array for the assay of benzaldehyde derivatives.

ce:section.ce:para: Benzaldehyde derivatives are one of the most important organic compounds. They can be applied as food additives, plasticizer, lubricant, pharmaceutica and so on [1–3]. Recent research reveals that excessive benzaldehyde derivatives in environment can cause severe healthy and environmental problems. Many countries have listed them as first-control toxic substances and closely monitor their ambient level [4,5]. Various analytical methods for benzaldehyde derivatives have emerged as the times require, including spectral, chromatographic and electrochemical methods [6,7]. However, most of these methods are limited by expensive equipment or complicated operating procedures. It is necessary to develop a simple, rapid, sensitive and low-cost method to detect benzaldehyde derivatives.

Fluorescence detection is one of the most popular and economical analytical methods [8–12]. Fluorescence signal can be read quickly and handily through simple operation on fluorospectrophotometer. Extensive analytes have been determined by using fluorescence methods, including mineral salts, organic compounds and biomacromolecules [13–15]. Various fluorescence probes are developed for fluorescence detection, and the recent mushroom growth of nanotechnology has arouse considerable attention to fluorescence nano-probes. Carbon nanodots (CDs) are one of the rising stars among various fluorescence nano-probes [16–18]. CDs take their advantage of simple synthetic methods, abundant carbon sources and good biocompatibility compared with other fluorescence nano-probes [19–21]. The high photostability of CDs guarantees stable fluorescence signal output and accurate detection results, which makes them promising candidates for fluorescence detection [22–24].

There are very few studies about applying fluorescence methods to detect benzaldehyde derivatives. Most of benzaldehyde derivatives themselves are non- fluorescence emissive. Therefore, fluorescence probes are necessary for fluorescence determination of benzaldehyde derivatives. Wu et al. prepared Y2O3 fluorescence nano-probes and utilized their chemiluminescence characteristics to determine benzaldehyde vapour [25]. Shi et al. synthesized two kinds of lanthanide(III)-organic frameworks and found them selectively sensitive to trace amounts of benzaldehyde derivatives in organic solution [26]. However, fluorescence determination of benzaldehyde derivatives in aqueous solution is hardly reported, which has a very important practical significance.

In this research, three kinds of CDs were prepared as fluorescence nano-probes to determine benzaldehyde derivatives. Fluorescence quenching phenomenon appeared when quantitative benzaldehyde derivatives were added into CD solution. There existed a linear correlation curve about the fluorescence intensity of CDs and the concentration of benzaldehyde derivatives. The concentration of benzaldehyde derivatives could be determined through monitoring the variation of the fluorescence intensity of CDs. Furthermore, sensor array was constructed using these CDs, and principal component analysis method (PCA) was applied to process the cumulative response of benzaldehyde derivatives from all of the CDs. Five kinds of benzaldehyde derivatives, including 2,4-dihydroxybenzaldehyde, salicylaldehyde, m-hydroxybenzaldehyde, benzaldehyde and m-methylbenzaldehyde, could be effectively discriminated and determined in the PCA scattergraph generated by the CD array. Benzaldehyde derivatives might form Schiff base bonds with CDs and further affect the photoluminescence (PL) behaviour of CDs through a static quenching mechanism. The possible sensing mechanism is shown in the following scheme (Scheme 1 ).

Salicylaldehyde, m-methylbenzaldehyde, glutaraldehyde, butyraldehyde, heptanal and m-hydroxybenzaldehyde were purchased from Beijing Innochem Technology Limited Company. Citric acid, urea, ethylenediamine, alanine, benzaldehyde and 2,4-dihydroxybenzaldehyde were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation. Anhydrous ethanol, NaOH and other reagents were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Company Limited (Shanghai, China). All the reagents were used as received without any other further purification. Deionized water was used in all experiments.

Photoluminescence measurements were executed on a fluorescence spectrophotometer (RF-5301PC, SHIMADZU, Japan). UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded on UV–vis spectrophotometer (TU-1900, Purkinje General, China). The X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were recorded using a K-alpha spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, UK). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) test was applied to observe the morphology of the CDs, which was carried out on a JEM-2100 (JEOL, Japan). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a Perkin-Elmer FT-IR spectrometer with KBr pellets. The fluorescence lifetime was measured using Fluorolog (Horiba Jobin Yvon) with a TCSPC system.

Both CD1 and CD2 were prepared via microwave heating method [27,28]. The carbon source and reaction conditions were different. For CD1, alanine (0.5 g) and urea (1.2 g) were dissolved in NaOH solution (15 mL, 0.5 M). For CD2, citric acid (1 g) and urea (1 g) were dissolved in 10 mL deionized water. After incubating in a domestic microwave oven for 4 min at 700 W, the solution turned light yellow and CDs were generated. CD3 was prepared using hydro-thermal method [29]. Citric acid (0.42 g) and ethylenediamine (0.54 mL) were dissolved in 10 mL deionized water. The solution was placed in a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave (25 mL) and incubated at 200 °C for 5 h to obtain CD solution. All the crude CDs were purified through centrifugation and dialysis methods. Centrifugation (12 000 rpm, 5 min) can remove undissolved substance in CD solution, while dialysis (dialysis membrane molecular weight cut off = 100, Spectrum Labs, USA) can remove precursors and unreacted carbon source. The as-prepared CDs were finally kept in freeze-dried powder.

In order to minimize the concentration effect of CDs, the absorbance in all of the CD solution was adjusted to 0.1 at their optimum excitation wavelength. The optimum excitation wavelength was the excitation wavelength where the fluorescence emission intensity of CDs reaches the maximum (λCD1 = 320 nm; λCD2 = 360 nm; λCD3 = 360 nm). Benzaldehyde derivatives in ethanol solution (10 μL) with a certain concentration was added into 3 mL CD aqueous solution. After about 1 min, this system had reacted strictly and steadily (Fig. S1). And the PL spectra were recorded after mixing well. The concentration of benzaldehyde derivatives in assay system was determined as 10 mM unless otherwise specified.

UV–vis spectra can provide important structural information about CDs (Fig. 2 ). The absorption at the range of 200–250 nm is the classical absorption band of CDs, representing the π–π* transition of several kinds of unsaturated bonds. The molecular vibration and rotation of the substituents on aromatic hydrocarbon contributes the absorption at 210–240 nm. The structure of CD3 is different from that of CD1 and CD2, mainly reflecting in the absorption after 300 nm. The absorption at 350 nm is probably caused by the n–π* transition of the unsaturated bonds containing hetero atoms. The absorption of CD2 extends from ultraviolet region to visible region. The absorption at ca. 400 nm may be assigned to the π–π* transition of porphyrin structure [36]. CDs with different structure are generated due to complicated factors, including different carbon source, synthetic methods, and forming mechanism. The difference in the structure of CDs will finally lead to the difference in their PL.

The PL spectra of CDs under various excitation wavelengths are presented in Fig. 3 . The fluorescence intensity of CDs increases first and then decreases after reaching a maximum value. The maximum PL emission wavelength is labeled as λmax, and their corresponding excitation wavelength is called the optimum excitation wavelength and labeled as λopt. The λmax and λopt of various CDs are different (CD1: λmax/λopt = 350 nm/320 nm; CD2: λmax/λopt = 450 nm/360 nm; CD3: λmax/λopt = 450 nm/380 nm). CD1 and CD2 show excitation-depended PL behavior, and the PL emission red-shifts with the increase of excitation wavelength. Excitation-depended PL phenomenon is very common for CD materials, which is most likely caused by quantum confinement effect [37,38]. Excitation-independed CDs, such as CD3, have also been reported in several literatures [39,40]. Various heating modes and reaction time contribute different carbonization degrees of CDs, which may cause the change of their PL behaviour [41]. However, adequate evidence is needed for this conclusion. The TEM images in Fig. S2 show the basic morphology and size of three CDs. They are almost spherical with a uniform grain-size. The grain diameter of CD1, CD2 and CD3 are about 9, 3, and 4 nm, respectively. The high resolution TEM (HRTEM) images (Fig. S2, inset) clearly shows all of these three CDs possess a graphite-like structure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PL response of CDs to benzaldehyde derivatives
ce:section.ce:para: Aliphatic or aromatic amine can form Schiff base bonds with carbonyl compounds through nucleophilic addition reactions under mild conditions [42–44]. Benzaldehyde derivatives possess aldehyde group, which can react with the amino-groups of CDs and form Schiff base bonds [45,46]. The binding of benzaldehyde derivatives can affect the PL behavior of CDs. The formation of the Schiff base bonds between the CDs and benzaldehyde derivatives can be confirmed from FT-IR spectrum (Fig. S3). There exist the characteristic stretching vibrations of the imine structure of Schiff base in the FT-IR spectrum of CD1 and salicyaldehyde, including the stretching vibration of CN (1639 cm−1) and the stretching vibration of NH (3300 cm−1). Instead, there are no such characteristic stretching vibrations in the FT-IR spectrum of CD1 without salicyaldehyde.

Fig. 4 shows the PL response of CDs to various benzaldehyde derivatives with the same concentration. The fluorescence intensity of CDs decreases in varying degrees with five benzaldehyde derivatives, and the array assay of benzaldehyde derivatives can be realized on this basis. Three CDs have different structure and exterior functional groups. According to the high-resolution XPS N 1s spectra of the CDs (Fig. 1), the amino-group content of CD1, CD2 and CD3 can be calculated as 80.06%, 77.61% and 74.32%, respectively, through dividing the peak area of NH spectrum to that of the whole N1s spectrum. Hence, CD1 have the most amino groups, while CD3 have the least. The more amino-groups the CDs have, the more Schiff-base bonds will form. More Schiff-base bonds would result in a higher fluorescence quenching efficiency of the CDs. Just as we speculate, in Fig. 4, the fluorescence quenching efficiency of CD1 is higher than that of CD2 and CD3. Five benzaldehyde derivatives also own some particular functional groups on the basis of benzaldehyde structure. These structural characteristics can influence the PL of CDs through the donor-acceptor electronic effect after the Schiff-base complex formation. Nevertheless, some non-covalent interactions, such as steric hindrance, electrostatic action, hydrogen bond, hydrophobic effects, or a combination of some of these, could also affect the final interaction between various CD-sensors and benzaldehyde-analytes. Hence, it is also difficult to explain the difference in reactivity between different benzaldehyde derivatives.

With the increase of the concentration of benzaldehyde derivatives, the fluorescence intensity of CDs decreases until holding the line (Fig. 5 ). There exists a good linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of CDs and the concentration of benzaldehyde derivatives within a certain concentration range (Fig. 5). Quantitative assay of benzaldehyde derivatives can be realized through monitoring the fluorescence intensity variation of CDs. The full validation of the assays, including the concentration range, linear equation, R-square and detection limit, are presented in Table S1, S2 and S3. The measureable concentration range covers from μM level to mM level, and the minimum detection limit can reach 0.3 μM. Compared with some other relevant literatures about the assay of benzaldehyde derivatives, the CD-based sensing system show a broad concentration range (0.3 μM–7.0 mM). A CD sensor could simultaneously respond to five kinds of benzaldehyde derivatives. Moreover, there's no need for complicated procedures and expensive instruments in the CD-based sensing system.

The fluorescence lifetime of CDs in the presence or absence of benzaldehyde derivatives was investigated to explore the quenching mechanism. The fluorescence lifetime of CDs remains unchanged after adding benzaldehyde derivatives (Fig. S4), indicating a static quenching mechanism [47,48]. A ground-state non-fluorescent complex may be formed between CDs and benzaldehyde derivatives.

Three CDs produce various fluorescence response to five kinds of benzaldehyde derivatives, which can be applied to construct the unique figureprints of benzaldehyde derivatives (Fig. 6 A). The figureprints of benzaldehyde derivatives reflect the cumulative responses from all of the CDs, and can be applied to dicriminate and recognize benzaldehyde derivatives. The multi-dimensional response pattern (3 sensors × 5 benzaldehyde derivatives × 6 trails) was built up after six duplicate tests [49]. These data were further statistically analysed using the principal component analysis method (PCA) by the software of SPSS Statistics [50,51]. PCA is a typical statistical procedure, which can convert original variables into linearly uncorrelated variables (principal components, PCs) using orthogonal transformation method [52,53]. PCs with eigenvalues greater than 1 are statistically significant based on Kaiser rule, and there exist only two PCs available in the as-prepared CD array [54]. These two PCs contribute 99.22% of the total variability (PC1: Proportion = 65.03%, Eigenvalue = 1.951; PC2: Proportion = 34.19%, Eigenvalue = 1.026), which confirms the important roles of these two PCs [55]. The correlation formulas of PC1 and PC2 are listed as follows.
(1) PC1 = −[CD1] * 18.2% + [CD2] * 42.2% + [CD3] * 66.5%
(2) PC2 = [CD1] * 82.3% + [CD2] * 28.6% − [CD3] * 27.6%

PC1 and PC2 are applied as the x-coordinate and y-coordinate to plot the PCA scattergraph about the benzaldehyde derivatives in Fig. 6B. All of the data points from the same benzaldehyde derivative stay together, and can be clearly distinguished from those of the other benzaldehyde derivatives. A circle is used to mark the exclusive zone of a kind of benzaldehyde derivatives, and data points appearing in this region are most likely such benzaldehyde derivatives. Five benzaldehyde derivatives can be intuitively discrinminated utlizing the scattergraph. Furthermore, we explored the working performance of the sensor array in the presence of various benzaldehyde derivatives, in order to figure out the optimal application concentration range of the sensor array (Fig. S5). Under 0.5–50 mM of benzaldehyde derivatives, the CD-based sensor array show a good discriminative performance for five benzaldehyde derivatives. The fluorescence response of CDs to various benzaldehyde derivatives hold the line in the presence of greater than or equal to 50 mM of benzaldehyde derivatives (Fig. 5). This demonstrates that the figureprints and scattergraphs of benzaldehyde derivatives (>50 mM) will remain the same to those at 50 mM. In conclusion, these results confirm the as-constructed sensor array can perform well in a wide concentration range of benzaldehyde derivatives (≥0.5 mM).

The practicability of the CD array in the determination of benzaldehyde derivatives was further evaluated through double blind experiments and background interference experiments. In the double blind experiments, two benzaldehyde derivatives were randomly selected from the previous five. The normalized fluorescence intensity of CDs with these two benzaldehyde derivatives was substituted in Eqs. (1) and (2) respectively, and plotted in the PCA scattergraph (Fig. 7 A). BD-1 fell into the exclusive zone of 2,4-dihydroxybenzaldehyde, while BD-2 fell into that of m-methylbenzaldehyde. Therefore, BD-1 and BD-2 could be determined as 2,4-dihydroxybenzaldehyde and m-methylbenzaldehyde just as designed. In the background interference experiments, local running water was chosen as the background solution to determine benzaldehyde derivatives. As is known, running water is susceptible to biological or chemical contamination, and especially contains various metal ions. Most of these substances can influence the PL behavior of CDs more or less. Five benzaldehyde derivatives can still be well discriminated in running water (Fig. 7B). Some aldehyde compounds without aromatic structure could interfere the experimental results. Hence, three aliphatic aldehydes, including glutaraldehyde, butyraldehyde, and heptanal, were added in the testing solution to further evaluate the anti-interference capability of the as-prepared sensor array. The sensor array could perform as well as before (Fig. S6).

In summary, novel fluorescent nano-probes based on CDs were developed for the assay of benzaldehyde derivatives. The CDs could form Schiff base bonds with benzaldehyde derivatives through the Schiff base reactions between the amino groups on CDs and the aldehyde groups on benzaldehyde derivatives. The linkage of benzaldehyde derivatives could result in the fluorescence quenching of CDs. Quantitative assay of benzaldehyde derivatives in a certain concentration range could be realized through monitoring the fluorescence intensity variation of CDs. Moreover, various benzaldehyde derivatives could cause the fluorescence quenching of CDs with varying degrees. Therefore, CD array was constructed for the further determination of benzaldehyde derivatives. Five benzaldehyde derivatives could be well discriminated and confirmed in the PCA scattergraph. This work provides a simple, rapid, sensitive and low-cost method to determine benzaldehyde derivatives in aqueous solution. We anticipate the CD array can be utilized in a wide range of areas.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51403093 and 51373073) and Natural Science Foundation for Education Department of Liaoning Province of China No. LYB201603.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.01.160.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yingying Li is working for her MS degree from the Department of chemistry, majoring in Polymer Chemistry and Physics, Liaoning University. She is interested in the preparation of carbon nanomaterials and design sensor arrays.

Xue Liu is an associate professor at the College of chemistry, Liaoning University, China. He received her PhD degree from the college of chemistry, NanKai University, China in 2013. Now, he is engaged in the preparation and application of the carbon nanomaterials.

Qiuhua Wu is an associate professor in College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, China. She received hPhD degree from College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, China in 2016. Now, she is engaged in the simulation of the biomineralization.
Jie Yi is working for his PhD degree, majoring in organic Chemistry, in Liaoning University. He focuses on preparation and application of the carbon nanomaterials.

Guolin Zhang is a professor in College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, China. He received his PhD degree from College of Chemistry, Nankai University, China in 2004. Now, he is engaged in Janus materials, carbon nanomaterials and sensors.
